regions.name: Moselle River Valley -2016
regions.excerpt: Where we fell in love with riesling...
regions.review:
Rachel had a work trip to Amsterdam. I decided to tag along. And instead of doing what most people do — stay in the city, hit the tourist spots — we asked ourselves a simple question: what's within driving distance? One Google search later, I saw a photo of a breathtaking valley with a castle sitting on top of a hill, a river winding through tiny storybook towns, and vineyards climbing so high they didn't look real. I clicked the link and I couldn't stop reading. The place was called Cochem. We had to go.
There was only one wrinkle. The Moselle is Riesling country — and back in 2016, when I thought of Riesling I thought of one thing: sweet wine. The kind that sits in the back of the wine shop. Not exactly what gets you excited about a spontaneous road trip. But something told me to trust it. So we rented a car in Amsterdam, drove four hours through some of the most beautiful scenery I've ever seen, and checked into a little family-run B&B right on the river. A hundred euros a night. Top floor room. Everything we needed.
Oh — and Rachel had just found out she was expecting our second child. So I held down the fort and tasted for the both of us. She was a good sport about it.
What happened next changed how I think about wine.
We found a small winery doing tastings and sat down with three Rieslings — dry, off-dry, and sweet. And I sat there genuinely stunned. The dry Riesling was crisp, refreshing, sophisticated. Nothing like what I expected. The off-dry had just enough fruit to make it interesting without tipping into sweetness. And the sweet? Even that was elegant — more like a Sauterne than the sugary stuff I'd written off years ago. I never thought I would look at a Riesling the way I look at a Sauvignon Blanc or a Sancerre. That afternoon I did. One grape. So many magical things.
We spent five days driving town to town through the valley. Every stop had different Rieslings with different personalities. Dry for early evening on the terrace. A touch sweeter with dinner. And here's the thing — I felt like a champion every morning. No heaviness, no fog. That's the high acidity in Riesling — it's as refreshing the next day as it is in the glass.
The vineyards themselves are something you have to see to believe. The Moselle has the steepest vineyard slopes of any wine region in Europe. So steep that harvesting by hand is nearly impossible — they use these horizontal rail carts to move along the hillside because no one can safely stand on the incline. When you see it from the valley floor, it's breathtaking.
And the best part? This region is accessible and affordable in a way that very few wine destinations are. You don't need a big budget to do this right.
Travel Tips:
Plan for at least 3 days — the towns are small and the pace is slow, and that's exactly the point. Start in Cochem and work your way through the valley. The biggest city is Trier, the oldest city in all of Germany, and well worth a stop. The journey ends beautifully in Koblenz, where the Moselle meets the Rhine. Keep in mind that English isn't widely spoken in the smaller towns, so come with patience and an open heart. The food is very German — hearty, unfussy, and honest. Embrace it. This region doesn't put on a show for tourists, and that's what makes it so special.
The Kazmi Take: Go. Don't overthink it, don't wait for the perfect moment. Rent a car, book a little B&B on the river, and drive from town to town. The Moselle will meet you with open arms.

regions.name: Port & The Duoro Valley - 2017
regions.review:
After the Mosel, something shifted for Rachel and me. We'd opened a door, and we couldn't stop walking through it. As our love for wine grew, so did our love for one specific corner of it — port. Big, generous, soulful. The kind of bottle that doesn't ask for attention but rewards you when you give it. By the time we started planning our next adventure, the answer felt obvious: Portugal.
Easy flight straight into Lisbon, a quick few hours up to Porto, and you're in port country. We flew in, rented a car, and drove three hours of jaw-dropping scenery to the center of the city. We rented a small, modest apartment — clean, central, €110 a night. Everything we needed.
Then we went on our adventures.
If you don't know Porto, here's the trick: all the legendary port houses — Graham's, Taylor's, Croft, Sandeman, the names you've stared at in your local wine shop for years — sit across the river in Vila Nova de Gaia, maybe a 20-minute walk from the city center. They line the hillside like a little kingdom of barrel rooms and dark wood and patient time. We must have hit twenty different port houses in three days. Tawnies at 10, 20, 30, and 40 years. Vintage ports. LBVs. Whites and rosés we didn't even know existed. By the end of it I could read a port label like a sentence.
Graham's was the standout. If you're going to do one well, do it there.
And the evenings — that's the other half of the trip. Cobblestone streets that go on forever, restaurants that quietly know what they're doing, octopus and seafood and sausages and bread that ruined other bread for me. Hospitality you don't have to earn. Affordability that almost felt like a mistake. We were a young couple starting out in our life, and Porto met us exactly where we were — generous, real, no pretension. Most nights we ended the same way: chocolate soufflé and a sweet tawny. Our thing.
But we couldn't come this close to the Douro Valley and not see it.
So we got back in the car and drove up. No one really tells you what "up" means until you're doing it — until you crest the first hill and the valley opens up beneath you and the vineyards come into view. Steep terraces carved into the hillsides, the river winding through it all, the curves of the road tracing the contours of the landscape. It looked like Bob Ross had sketched it himself. At one point the road was so narrow and steep my naive self figured it was a one way, wrong, you never know your driving skills until you are reversing down a windy gravel road with no rails and a bus is following you down staring into you soul. Luckily we lived to tell about it. Nonetheless it was worth it because the Douro is a UNESCO World Heritage site, and ten minutes in, you understand exactly why.
We didn't make any appointments. We just pulled off where the signs told us to. At least three wineries opened their doors and welcomed us in like we were old friends. We sat high above the river and tasted what the Douro actually is — not just port, but a whole world of native red grapes most people have never heard of. Touriga Nacional. Touriga Franca. Tinta Roriz. Young vintages, older ones, a few sweet wines (still not our favorite, but in that valley, sitting where they were grown — even those felt right). We left with our eyes opened and a list of grapes we knew we'd one day carry in our cellar. We do now.
And here's what I keep coming back to: sometimes it's not just about the wine. It's about the place. It's about the people. It's about looking out at something and knowing the beauty in your glass can only really be understood while you're sitting in its presence.
Travel Tips:
Portugal is Western European, but unlike Paris or London, it's more rugged — and that's part of the charm. English isn't as readily spoken outside the cities, so come with an open mind and an open heart. They'll meet you with open arms. Spend at least three days in Porto and don't shortchange the port lodges — go to the big names, take the tours, taste the older tawnies. Then drive up to the Douro. Don't overthink it; the wineries that matter will let you in.
The Kazmi Take: Worth every penny. Cost-effective, picturesque, and one of the most welcoming wine trips we've ever taken. Go.
